definition: A service that routes your web requests through another server.
A proxy that sits between a client and the internet, forwarding client requests to destination websites.
A proxy that sits in front of origin servers and receives external traffic on their behalf.
An additional proxy layer used by another proxy system to forward traffic further upstream.
Ghost's websocket tunnel endpoint used by transports for proxied requests.
The backend endpoint that accepts proxied tunnel traffic for Ghost transports.
Ghost's beta remote desktop client for connecting to a host PC from the browser.
The request backend implementation (for example LibCurl or Epoxy) used by the proxy engine.
A transport backend that uses curl-style networking behavior for HTTP/HTTPS requests through Ghost.
A Ghost transport backend used to relay proxied traffic through the configured Wisp path.
A legacy socket proxy protocol that forwards TCP traffic without modern authentication features.
An advanced socket proxy protocol supporting authentication and broader proxying capabilities than SOCKS4.
A proxy endpoint that accepts HTTP-style proxy requests and forwards them to destination hosts.
The mode that decides whether Ghost uses direct routing or an additional remote proxy endpoint.
Routing mode where Ghost sends traffic through its normal configured backend path without a user-specified remote proxy endpoint.
A user-provided proxy endpoint (HTTP, SOCKS4, or SOCKS5) used as an extra routing layer for Ghost traffic.
The internal engine path Ghost uses to process requests, such as Ultraviolet or Scramjet flows.
A proxy backend path used by Ghost for encoded request forwarding through service worker routing.
A proxy backend/runtime used by Ghost that rewrites and relays web traffic through worker-based handling.
The connection layer used to configure and switch Ghost transport handlers for proxied traffic.
A worker/client channel used by the proxy stack to perform outbound network requests.
A browser worker that intercepts and handles network requests, often used by proxy frameworks in Ghost.
A persistent communication path used to encapsulate and relay traffic between endpoints.
A full-duplex protocol for persistent client-server communication, used by Wisp transport channels.
Transport Layer Security, the protocol used to encrypt HTTPS and secure WebSocket (WSS) traffic.
A connection failure raised when TLS/SSL handshake or certificate validation cannot be completed for a request.
A signed cryptographic document used to verify domain identity for secure HTTPS/TLS connections.
Domain Name System, which resolves human-readable domains into IP addresses.
The domain label portion of a URL that identifies the destination server.
A concrete network address (URL/host/port path) that a client connects to for a service.
The combination of scheme, host, and port that defines a security boundary in browsers.
A browser security model restricting interactions between documents/resources from different origins.
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, an HTTP policy mechanism that allows or blocks cross-origin requests.
The client identifier string sent with requests, often describing browser and platform.
Metadata sent with an HTTP request, such as authorization, user agent, or content preferences.
Metadata returned by a server with the response, including caching, security, and content details.
The delay between sending a request and receiving a response, typically measured in milliseconds.
A configured maximum wait duration after which a network request is aborted as failed.
A repeated request attempt after a failure, often used for transient network issues.
An alternate transport/backend path used when the preferred path fails.
A filter mechanism that removes or suppresses known ad resources and ad-related page elements.
A control that prevents websites from opening new popup windows/tabs without user intent.
A control that prevents download-triggering actions and file-like navigation targets.
An internal route using the `ghost://` scheme to navigate built-in Ghost pages and tools.
Ghost's internal placeholder URL used for New Tab state before external page navigation.
The combined address/search input bar used to navigate URLs and submit search queries.
